steps.title: Solve both quadratics using the quadratic formula
steps.content:
For $x^2 + 6x + 7 = 0$, the discriminant is $\Delta = 36 - 28 = 8$:
$$r, s = \frac{-6 \pm \sqrt{8}}{2} = \frac{-6 \pm 2\sqrt{2}}{2} = -3 \pm \sqrt{2}$$
For $x^2 + 8x + 8 = 0$, the discriminant is $\Delta = 64 - 32 = 32$:
$$t, u = \frac{-8 \pm \sqrt{32}}{2} = \frac{-8 \pm 4\sqrt{2}}{2} = -4 \pm 2\sqrt{2}$$

steps.title: Form the new roots and verify their sum
steps.content:
With $r = -3 - \sqrt{2}$, $s = -3 + \sqrt{2}$, $t = -4 - 2\sqrt{2}$, $u = -4 + 2\sqrt{2}$:
$$r + t = -7 - 3\sqrt{2}, \quad s + u = -7 + 3\sqrt{2}$$
Their sum is $(r+t) + (s+u) = -14$, confirming the $x$ coefficient in $x^2 + 14x + c = 0$.

steps.title: Calculate c as the product of the new roots
steps.content:
$$(r+t)(s+u) = (-7 - 3\sqrt{2})(-7 + 3\sqrt{2})$$
This is a difference of squares $(a-b)(a+b) = a^2 - b^2$ with $a = -7$ and $b = 3\sqrt{2}$:
$$= 49 - 9 \cdot 2 = 49 - 18 = 31$$
Therefore, $c = 31$.
